It is 11:00 on a Tuesday. The vendor on your project (an integration provider) has just emailed you to say their team will not deliver the integration plan today, despite a written commitment that it would be ready by close of business. They are now asking for an extension to next Wednesday — eight days late.

This affects three things:
1. Raj, the test lead on your team, was planning to start dependent test-prep work tomorrow morning based on that plan.
2. Helen, your PM, owns the relationship with the vendor and has a vendor sync booked for Thursday.
3. The sponsor, David Sharma, sees the project's RAG status weekly. He has not been emailed directly by you before. Helen has briefed him verbally on the integration workstream once.

You need to produce three short pieces — total combined length 350-700 words — to handle the situation in writing this morning. Each is to a different audience and medium:

1. **Slack to Raj** — peer, daily collaborator. He needs to know now so he can decide whether to start the dependent work, defer it, or shift to other tasks.

2. **Email to Helen (PM)** — your manager, who needs to know what you're doing about it and whether you need her to step in. She will likely raise it with the vendor on Thursday.

3. **Email to David Sharma (Sponsor)** — senior, has not been emailed directly by you before. He needs a brief, dignified factual note so he is not surprised if the slip surfaces in the steering report.

Apply the chapter's principles. Lede first in every piece. Strip the hedges. Do not apologize for doing your job. Modulate the register — Slack should not read like a sponsor note, and the sponsor note should not read like Slack.

Write all three pieces in a single document. Label each (e.g. "PIECE 1 — SLACK TO RAJ"). Include subject lines for the two emails.
